sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1. The board shows a gray background with two light gray rectangular panels separated by a vertical purple bar. Left panel has a dark square outline and a small light green square. Right panel has a dark square outline. There's also a small green square near the center. Need to understand the goal and mechanics.
- Plan: Inspect segmentation to identify all objects and their positions, then probe with movement actions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the changes after RIGHT:
- Node 6 (light green square) moved from (30-32, 18-20) to (30-32, 21-23) - shifted right by 3
- Node 7 changed from light green (N) to white (w) - it was at (30-32, 27-29), now white at (30-32, 24-26)
- Node 8 changed from white to light green (N) - was at (30-32, 30-32), now light green at (30-32, 27-29)
- Node 9 changed from white (W) 1x1 to white (w) 3x3 - was at (31, 19), now 3x3 at (30-32, 30-32)
- Node 10 changed from black (B) 1x1 to white (W) 1x1 - was at (31, 28), now at (31, 22)
- Node 11 changed from black (B) 1x1 to... wait, let me re-read
Actually the IDs shifted because new nodes were created. Let me track by color and position:
Before RIGHT:
- Green hollow square with white center at (30-32, 18-20) [Node 6+9]
- Green hollow square with black center at (30-32, 27-29) [Node 7+10]
- White 3x3 solid at (30-32, 30-32) [Node 8]
After RIGHT:
- Green hollow square with white center at (30-32, 21-23) [Node 6+10] - moved right 3
- White 3x3 solid at (30-32, 24-26) [Node 7] - was green, now white
- Green hollow square with black center at (30-32, 27-29) [Node 8+11] - was white, now green
- White 3x3 solid at (30-32, 30-32) [Node 9] - was white 1x1, now 3x3
So it seems like RIGHT shifts the entire row of objects to the right. The green squares and white squares are shifting right, with the colors/properties following.
Wait, let me reconsider. The objects seem to be on a track/row. RIGHT moves them all right by 3 columns.
The goal might be to get the green squares (with their colored centers) into the dark square outlines.
Left panel dark square: at (32-36, 11-15) - center at about (34, 13)
Right panel dark square: at (26-30, 44-48) - center at about (28, 46)
The green squares are at row 30-32, so they're on the same horizontal band.
Let me think about what needs to happen:
- The green square with white center needs to go into one of the dark outlines
- The green square with black center needs to go into the other
But wait - the dark square on the left is at row 32-36, which is lower than the green squares at row 30-32. And the right one is at row 26-30.
Let me try more RIGHT actions to see the pattern, or try UP/DOWN to move vertically.
Actually, let me first try LEFT to undo and see if it reverses.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT shifts objects on the horizontal track rightward by 3 columns. The green hollow squares (with colored center pixels) and white squares move together as a chain. Two dark square outlines serve as targets - one on left panel at (32-36, 11-15) and one on right panel at (26-30, 44-48). Goal likely involves placing green squares into dark outlines.
Plan: Try LEFT to reverse, then explore UP/DOWN for vertical movement to align with target squares.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT to see if it reverses the RIGHT movement
result = action(['LEFT'])
print("Result:", result.get('action_display'), "gameplay_changed:", result.get('gameplay_changed'))
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nSmall objects after LEFT:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
